title_zh: 马塞尔·杜尚毁掉了艺术吗？
rubric_en: If you hate contemporary art, everything you dislike about it can be traced back to him.
rubric_zh: 如果你讨厌当代艺术，那么你不喜欢的一切都可以追溯到他。

paragraphs.en: “Bicycle Wheel,” 1951 (Museum of Modern Art, New York / © Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York / ADAGP, Paris / Association Marcel Duchamp)
paragraphs.zh: “自行车轮”（Bicycle Wheel），1951年（纽约现代艺术博物馆 / © Artists Rights Society（ARS），纽约 / ADAGP，巴黎 / 马塞尔·杜尚协会）

paragraphs.en: Something about art disgusted Marcel Duchamp. Expression, taste, aesthetic intention—anything that gave off a whiff of the precious, he recoiled from. He was modern. He relished the impersonal operations of chance. He loved jokes and sex and the movements of modern machinery. “Painting is finished,” he said to the Romanian sculptor Constantin Brâncuși at an air show in Paris in 1912, when he was 25. “Who will do any better than that propeller?”
paragraphs.zh: 有些东西让马塞尔·杜尚反感艺术：表达、品味、美学意图——任何带着一丝“高雅”气息的东西，他都会本能地退缩。他是“现代派”。他喜欢偶然性的非人格化操作。他喜欢笑话、性爱和现代机械的运转。1912年在巴黎的一次航空展上，这位25岁的人对罗马尼亚雕塑家康斯坦丁·布兰库西说：“绘画已经完成了。” “谁还能把这玩意儿做得比螺旋桨更好吗？”

paragraphs.en: The next year, Duchamp mounted a bicycle wheel onto an upside-down fork fixed to a wooden stool. He enjoyed the way the spokes rotated around a central axis, like a propeller, and how they reflected shimmering light, like a fireplace. He wouldn’t come up with the term until two years later, but Duchamp had created the first “readymade.” The mischievous, machine-obsessed, maddeningly inconsistent Frenchman was on his way to having an impact on modern creativity comparable to that of Richard Wagner or Charles Baudelaire on an earlier generation of modernists.
paragraphs.zh: 第二年，杜尚把一个车轮装在倒置的车叉上，并把它固定在一张木凳上。他喜欢车辐像螺旋桨一样绕着中心轴旋转，以及它们反射出闪烁的光芒，像壁炉一样跳动。直到两年后他才提出“现成品”（readymade）这一说法，但杜尚已经创造了第一件“现成品”。这个顽皮、痴迷机器、又令人恼火地前后矛盾的法国人，正在走向对现代创造力的影响力，与过去一代现代主义者眼中的理查德·瓦格纳或夏尔·博德莱尔可比。

paragraphs.en: Duchamp left behind a legacy that people either love or loathe. He is known as the father of conceptual art, but his so-called ideas were mostly idle notions, provocations, speculations. Opinion divides on whether he snuffed out or emancipated art. But fret as we might about the fate of art after Duchamp, this strange, original man—so nonchalant! so fanatical!—was engaged in something more private, urgent, and amorous (more on that later) than merely “making art.”
paragraphs.zh: 杜尚留下了一个人们或爱或恨的遗产。他被视为概念艺术之父，但他所谓的“观念”大多只是空转的构想、挑衅和猜想。人们争论他是扼杀了艺术还是解放了艺术。尽管我们可能会因为杜尚之后艺术的命运而焦虑，这位古怪又原始、毫不在意又狂热的男人——不仅仅是“在做艺术”——他实际上投入了更多更私人、更紧迫、更充满暧昧情欲的事情（稍后再谈）。

paragraphs.en: A visit to the massive Duchamp exhibition at the Museum of Modern Art—his first North American retrospective in more than 50 years—can feel less like taking in a traditional exhibition than like wandering through an archive. (It was organized by MoMA’s curators Ann Temkin and Michelle Kuo, and Matthew Affron of the Philadelphia Museum of Art.) The items on display, many of them replicas sanctioned by the artist, have none of the aloof self-sufficiency, the “aura,” we expect from great art. Each object is instead more like a footnote or an index entry for something that once existed, was once thought, once got a laugh. The farther you advance, the more the show starts to feel forensic, like a crime scene. Near the end, your suspicions are confirmed: On display is a poster that Duchamp designed for his first-ever retrospective, at the Pasadena Art Museum in 1963. It features a mock WANTED notice offering a $2,000 reward. The mug shots are of himself.
paragraphs.zh: 参观纽约现代艺术博物馆规模宏大的杜尚展——这是他五十多年后在北美的首次回顾展——会让人感觉不像是在欣赏一场传统展览，更像是在浏览一个档案馆。(该展览由MoMA策展人安·坦金（Ann Temkin）和米歇尔·郭（Michelle Kuo）策划，并由费城艺术博物馆的马修·阿弗朗（Matthew Affron）协办。)展出的作品中，许多都是艺术家本人认可的复制品，却没有我们从伟大艺术中期待到的那种超然自足、那种“灵韵”与“光环”。每件作品更像是对某种曾经存在、曾被思考、曾引发笑声之物的脚注或目录项。你越往里走，展览越显得像一场法医调查，像一个犯罪现场。到了后段，你的猜想便得到印证：展厅里陈列着杜尚为1963年在帕萨迪纳艺术博物馆举办的第一次回顾展设计的一张海报。海报上有一个戏仿的“寻人启事”式告示，悬赏2,000美元。照片中的“嫌疑人”就是他本人。

paragraphs.en: If you dislike contemporary art, all of the things you hate about it can be traced back to Duchamp. Art that requires no skill or that was made, conversely, with great skill by people other than the “artist” claiming credit for it. Art that is all backstory, that leans so heavily on verbal explanations that it would collapse in a heap without them. Art tailored to generate pretentious academic dissertations. Art as cult fodder, marginalia, obscurantism—Duchamp inaugurated all of this.
paragraphs.zh: 如果你不喜欢当代艺术，那么你厌恶它的所有内容，都可以追溯到杜尚。比如要求观者不需要技艺，或者反过来说，由“艺术家”之外的人创作却又让“艺术家”本人领取功劳的作品；比如全部依赖背景故事、又高度倚重语言阐释以至于一旦去掉解释就会土崩瓦解的作品；比如专为写作空洞而炫耀的学术论文而量身定做的艺术；比如成为教派口号、边注、晦涩言谈之源的艺术——这些都由杜尚开创。

paragraphs.en: Yet his work has a lively, quicksilver quality that continues to feel generative. You can sense his wicked intelligence flickering behind some of the most rebellious, winningly unselfserious impulses in contemporary art. He’s so funny. Call me puerile, but I still laugh at his reproduction of the Mona Lisa, on which he drew a mustache and goatee in pencil. He called the piece L.H.O.O.Q.; the letters, spoken individually in French, sound like Elle a chaud au cul (“She has a hot ass”).
paragraphs.zh: 然而他的创作依旧有一种鲜活、敏捷如汞的特质，持续不断地激发新的可能性。你可以在当代艺术一些最叛逆、又毫不做作地机智幽默的冲动背后，感到他邪魅般的聪明在闪烁。他太滑稽了。也许你会说这很幼稚，但我仍会笑着看他那幅《蒙娜丽莎》复制品：他用铅笔给她画上小胡子和山羊胡。他把这件作品叫作《L.H.O.O.Q.》；这几个字母单独读出来在法语里像“Elle a chaud au cul”（她的屁股很热/很性感）。

paragraphs.en: He denied being an influence on anyone, but in truth his artistic progenies are everywhere. Artists who use readymades aren’t the only ones borrowing from Duchamp. Also in his debt are artists who incorporate chance; make assemblages and installations; play with alter egos and cross-dressing; present their own lives as works of art; embrace copies, replicas, factory-style production; mock the art market by presenting as “art” things that are clearly absurd or worthless (Yves Klein selling empty space, Piero Manzoni selling cans of his own excrement, Maurizio Cattelan selling a banana taped to a wall); make work involving machines, eroticism, molds of body parts, archives, graffiti, optical illusions, puns. The list of those who haven’t been influenced by Duchamp would likely be shorter.
paragraphs.zh: 他否认过自己影响了任何人，但实际上他的艺术后代随处可见。那些使用现成品（readymade）的艺术家并不是唯一借鉴杜尚的人。还有一大批受其影响的艺术家，他们会使用偶然性；做拼贴与装置；玩弄另一重身份和变装；把自己的生活当作艺术作品来展示；拥抱复制、仿制、工厂式生产；用把明显荒诞或毫无价值之物当作“艺术”来嘲讽艺术市场（例如Yves Klein出售“空白空间”、皮耶罗·曼索尼出售自己粪便罐头、毛里齐奥·卡特兰把一根香蕉胶带粘在墙上）；创作涉及机器、情色、身体部位模型、档案、涂鸦、视觉错觉、文字游戏的作品。那些不受杜尚影响的人名录，恐怕会更短。

paragraphs.en: Duchamp himself, one of seven children, grew up in an affluent household just outside Rouen, where “so much artistry” ran in his family, he later said, that it was like being immersed in an “aesthetic bath.” Though his father was a notary, his grandfather had been a successful artist. His mother, too, was an amateur artist in her youth, and his older brothers, Gaston and Raymond, gave up law and medicine, respectively, and became artists in Paris. Suzanne, one of his younger sisters, also became an artist.
paragraphs.zh: 杜尚本人是七个孩子中的一个，在鲁昂城外一个富裕家庭中长大。他后来提到，家里“艺术氛围”浓厚，几乎像是长期浸在一场“审美洗礼”里。尽管他的父亲是公证人，但祖父却是一位颇有成就的艺术家。他的母亲年轻时也曾是业余画家，而年长的两个哥哥加斯东和雷蒙则分别放弃了法律和医学，后来在巴黎成为画家。其妹妹苏珊娜，也是他较小的妹妹之一，也后来成为了艺术家。

paragraphs.en: From the beginning, Duchamp’s sense of his calling seemed to have had more to do with familial love than any kind of inner aesthetic conviction. “When I was 16, I thought for about six months that I’d like to be a notary like my father,” he later explained, “but that was just because I loved my father. I adored my brothers.” In 1904, he moved to Paris to join them.
paragraphs.zh: 从一开始，杜尚对自己天命的认同似乎更多源于家庭情感，而不是出于任何内在的审美信仰。他后来解释说：“我16岁时，差不多有半年时间以为自己想当一名像父亲一样的公证人，但那只是因为我爱我的父亲。我非常崇拜我的兄弟。”1904年，他来到巴黎与他们汇合。

paragraphs.en: The opening galleries at MoMA lead us briskly from the artist’s early satirical cartoons—their sly captions anticipating his later, punning titles—through a smattering of richly colored Cézanne- and Matisse-influenced paintings to his semi-abstract, Cubist works, all in shades of brown. Duchamp played chess starting in childhood. In the 1910s, he began to funnel a growing interest in systems of logic and machines (starting with coffee mills and chocolate grinders) into, strangely enough, a lifelong preoccupation with eroticism.
paragraphs.zh: MoMA 的开场展厅让观众从这位艺术家的早期讽刺漫画出发——这些漫画中的机敏题注预示了他后来的文字戏仿式标题——一路走向一些受塞尚与马蒂斯影响、色彩浓烈的画作，再到他半抽象、立体主义风格的棕色调作品。杜尚从童年起就开始下棋。到了1910年代，他把自己对逻辑体系和机械装置（先是从咖啡磨豆机和巧克力研磨机开始）的兴趣，奇妙地转化为对性欲主题的终身执着。

paragraphs.en: I think of Duchamp’s oeuvre, touched by the same World War I nihilism that produced Dada, as an attempt to generate inventive energy from the trauma of total war, exile, and modern conformity. But no one who delves into Duchamp can shake the feeling that erotic fixations and frustrated romantic love are at the core of his imaginative universe. How else to explain his focus on masturbation? He used his own semen in at least one artwork, used chocolate grinders as a metaphor for masturbation (“The bachelor grinds his chocolate himself”), and explained his enduring obsession with circles and rotation as “a kind of onanism.”
paragraphs.zh: 我把杜尚的全部创作看作在第一次世界大战所催生的虚无主义（也孕育了达达主义）影响下的一次尝试：从全面战争、流离失所和现代社会同质化的创伤中，生成一种创造性的能量。但任何深入研究杜尚的人都会揪住这样一种感觉：性冲动和受挫的浪漫爱情是他想象宇宙的核心。否则又该如何解释他对自慰主题的持续关注呢？他在至少一件作品中使用了自己的精液，把巧克力研磨机当作自慰的隐喻（“单身汉亲自研磨他的巧克力”），并把他对圆形和旋转的长期迷恋解释为“一种自我手淫式的行为”。

paragraphs.en: Duchamp’s life was marked by two serious cases of unrequited love. The object of the first was Gabriële Buffet-Picabia, whom Duchamp met when he was about 24. She promptly introduced him to her husband, Francis Picabia, a playboy and a pioneer of abstract art. Buffet-Picabia had studied avant-garde music in Paris and Berlin, and she now pushed Picabia and Duchamp into new approaches to visual art. Recognizing Duchamp’s paradoxical nature, she saw an anxious perfectionist, “possessed by a need for absolute logic,” and yet also a rebel who embraced randomness. The trio enjoyed in-jokes, road trips in fast cars (Picabia was obsessed with automobiles), and artistic experiments. In Gabriële, a biographical novel about Buffet-Picabia, their great-grandmother, Ann and Claire Berest describe Picabia and Duchamp as sharing “the same taste for shattered icons, for the art of irony and the irony of art, for jokes no matter the circumstance, and the idea that God is dead.”
paragraphs.zh: 杜尚的一生经历了两段严重的单相思。第一位对象是加布里埃尔·布菲特-皮卡比亚（Gabriële Buffet-Picabia），他大约24岁时与她相识。她很快把他介绍给自己的丈夫弗朗西斯·皮卡比亚（Francis Picabia）——一位花花公子，也是抽象艺术的先驱。布菲特-皮卡比亚在巴黎和柏林学习了先锋音乐，她如今把皮卡比亚和杜尚都推向了视觉艺术的新方向。她看透了杜尚的矛盾性格，认为他是一个焦虑的完美主义者，“被对绝对逻辑的执念所驱使”，又同时是一个拥抱随机性的叛逆者。在这三人关系中，他们享受内圈玩笑、乘快车在路上兜风（皮卡比亚痴迷汽车），以及各种艺术实验。安妮和克莱尔·贝雷斯特（Ann and Claire Berest）在加布里埃尔的传记《加布里埃尔》中写道，皮卡比亚和杜尚共享“对破碎偶像的同好、对讽刺艺术与艺术讽刺的热爱、对任何情境都能讲笑话的想法，以及上帝已死的观念。”

paragraphs.en: Nude Descending a Staircase (No. 2), 1912 (Philadelphia Museum of Art, Louise and Walter Arensberg Collection / © Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York / ADAGP, Paris / Association Marcel Duchamp)
paragraphs.zh: 《一位裸体女人下楼梯（No. 2）》，1912年（费城艺术博物馆，Louise 与 Walter Arensberg 收藏 / © 纽约艺术家版权协会（ARS） / ADAGP，巴黎 / 马塞尔·杜尚协会）

paragraphs.en: Duchamp was taken aback when his painting of a nude woman descending a staircase, her movements captured in the manner of a superimposed sequence of stop-motion photographs, was presented at the 1913 Armory Show in New York and made him notorious. Nude Descending a Staircase (No. 2) was lampooned in the press (an “explosion in a shingle mill” was how one critic described it). But audiences had seen nothing like it, and, in a culture trying to make sense of onrushing modernity, it quickly became the most famous painting in America. Its reception encouraged Duchamp to travel to the United States in the summer of 1915. War was raging in Europe, and he stayed for three years. A woman friend said he was “lionized by tout New York and courted by most of the female population.”
paragraphs.zh: 当1913年在纽约阿莫里艺术展（Armory Show）上展出他那幅描绘一位裸体女子下楼梯的画作时，杜尚颇为震惊：人物动作被表现为定格照叠加序列式的运动轨迹。这幅《降落楼梯的裸体者（2号）》在新闻界遭到讥讽（有评论家将其形容为“瓦屋厂里的爆炸”）。但观众此前从未见过类似作品，在一个急于理解飞速推进的现代性的文化氛围中，它迅速成为美国最著名的画作之一。这种反响促使杜尚在1915年夏天前往美国，并停留了三年。一个女性友人说，他在纽约“受到各界追捧，受到几乎所有女性人群的热烈追逐”。

paragraphs.en: Many experts, though, believe he was allegorizing his unfulfilled longing for his best friend’s wife in the masterpiece he embarked on during those years, The Bride Stripped Bare by Her Bachelors, Even, which he continued to labor over for eight more and left poignantly unfinished. The Large Glass, as it’s more commonly called, consists of two glass panels set vertically in a metal frame. The glass is inscribed—in lead wire, lead foil, oil, varnish, and dust—with diagrammatic imagery evoking machinery. The machines represent a bride (above) and nine bachelors (below). When the glass cracked during transportation, Duchamp put the pieces back together, relishing the random-looking lines of breakage (it is now permanently on display in the Philadelphia Museum of Art). He planned to have the work accompanied by a book-length guide to the abstruse and self-involved thinking that went into it, but ended up settling for an unbound pile of notes called The Green Box.
paragraphs.zh: 然而，许多专家认为，他在这几年里创作的《新娘脱去丈夫们的衣装》（The Bride Stripped Bare by Her Bachelors, Even）这件杰作，则是在寓言化他对挚友妻子的未果渴望，并且他后来又在其上反复劳动了八年，最终留下了令人心痛的未完成状态。该作常被称为《大玻璃》（The Large Glass），由两块垂直安放在金属框架中的玻璃板构成。玻璃上以铅丝、铅箔、油彩、清漆和尘埃“书写”出具有图解意味、仿佛机械装置的意象。该作品中的机器象征上方的新娘与下方的九个单身汉。玻璃在运输过程中破碎后，杜尚将碎片重新拼接，并欣赏其随机般出现的裂纹（现已在费城艺术博物馆永久展出）。他原本计划配一本文献级长度的说明书，来解释其中晦涩且自我指涉的思考过程，但最终只整理出一本未装订的笔记汇编，命名为《绿盒子》。

paragraphs.en: While he worked on The Large Glass, he produced a series of readymades with witty titles. The most famous appeared in 1917, when Duchamp turned a porcelain urinal on its back and signed it “R. Mutt.” Fountain, as he called the piece, is a provocation, a prank. But it’s also an homage to the wonders of modern plumbing. Two years earlier, three such urinals had been displayed at the Newark Museum, whose founding director, John Cotton Dana, pronounced that “the genius and skill which have gone into the adornment and perfecting of familiar household objects should receive the same recognition as do now the genius and skill of painting in oils.”
paragraphs.zh: 在创作《大玻璃》期间，他还制作了一系列带有俏皮题目的现成物。最著名的一件出现于1917年，当时杜尚把一只瓷制小便池倒置并签名为“R. Mutt”。他称这件作品为《喷泉》（Fountain），它是一种挑衅和恶作剧；但它同时也是对现代化排水系统奇迹的致敬。两年前，纽瓦克博物馆曾展出了三只这样的瓷质小便池，其创始馆长约翰·科顿·丹纳当时指出：“对我们日常生活中的家用物件进行装饰和完善所付出的天赋与技艺，理应得到与油画艺术创作者同等的认可。”

paragraphs.en: Amused by this way of thinking, Duchamp began to ridicule those who kept at painting, rebuking them for being obsessed with the smell of paint and deriding their efforts as “retinal art” (art that engages only the eye). He wanted to bring art back to what it had been during the Renaissance, when Leonardo da Vinci understood it as a cosa mentale—an intellectual matter. But Duchamp’s idea of “intellectual” was always gravitating toward the erotic: “I want to grasp things with the mind,” he said, “the way the penis is grasped by the vagina.”
paragraphs.zh: 受到这种观念的启发后，杜尚开始嘲讽仍在坚持写实绘画的人，指责他们沉迷于油漆气味般的外在手法，并贬低他们的努力，称其为“视网膜艺术”（只作用于眼睛的艺术）。他想把艺术带回文艺复兴时期的状态，因为当时列奥纳多·达·芬奇将其视为“cosa mentale”——一种思维性的事务。但杜尚所理解的“智性”始终带着明显的情色倾向。他说过：“我想用头脑去把握事物，就像阴茎被阴道所把握一样。”

paragraphs.en: In 1920, Duchamp decided to create a female alter ego, Rose Sélavy. Being “a lone individual with a masculine name,” he explained 40 years later, didn’t suffice, and he wanted “to make another personality from myself.” He changed the spelling to Rrose Sélavy, to make it sound more like Eros, c’est la vie. “Rose” specialized in facetious entrepreneurial gambits (supported by wealthy patrons, Duchamp enjoyed mocking the profit motive), optical illusions, and sexual innuendo.
paragraphs.zh: 1920年，杜尚决定创造一个女性性格的替身——罗丝·塞拉维（Rose Sélavy）。四十年后他解释说，成为“一个带有男性名字的单独个体”并不足够，他想“从我自己身上制造另一种人格”。他把名字改成Rrose Sélavy，让它更像“Eros, c’est la vie”（法语“爱欲就是生活”）的发音。罗丝擅长的是诙谐的“投机性”商业把戏（杜尚有得天独厚的富有赞助人背景，乐于嘲弄逐利动机）、视觉错觉以及性意味的双关语。

paragraphs.en: And then, three years later, having successfully overturned everyone’s idea of what art might be (a snow shovel! an ampoule of air! a portrait of the artist in drag!), Duchamp returned to Paris and, for more than a decade, dedicated himself to chess. He played in high-level tournaments, represented France at four Chess Olympiads, and even co-wrote a book on chess theory. But he continued to dabble in graphic design and tended to his legacy by creating elaborate portable boxes with mirrors and secret compartments, all filled with fastidiously executed small-scale reproductions of his bizarre but brilliant work. He settled in Manhattan during World War II but died in France in 1968.
paragraphs.zh: 三年后，当他已经成功颠覆了人们对艺术可能性的认知（一个雪铲！一支装气体的安瓿！一个“扮装”中的艺术家自画像！）后，杜尚回到巴黎，此后十多年将自己几乎全部投入到国际象棋中。他参加了高级别赛事，代表法国参加了四届国际象棋奥林匹克团体赛，甚至合著了一本关于象棋理论的书。然而他仍然时不时涉猎平面设计，并通过制作精致的便携箱盒（内含镜子和秘密隔层）来经营他的遗产，这些箱盒里装满了他那怪诞却出色的作品的小尺寸精细复制品。二战期间他定居曼哈顿，最终于1968年在法国去世。

paragraphs.en: A year later, the world learned that Duchamp had worked in secret for more than 20 years on a diorama-like work called Étant donnés (which will be on view when the show travels to the Philadelphia Museum of Art, where it resides). It consists of an old wooden door at the end of a corridor, with two peepholes, through which the viewer spies a brick wall with a large opening. Beyond this can be seen the cropped torso, along with an arm and legs, of a naked woman splayed corpse-like on branches and leaves, her shaved genitalia exposed, her left arm holding up a shining gas lamp.
paragraphs.zh: 一年后，世人得知杜尚已经秘密创作了二十余年一个仿景观模型般的作品《Étant donnés》（该作在展览巡展至费城艺术博物馆时展出，馆内现藏此作）。它由一扇走廊尽头的旧木门组成，木门上有两个窥视孔，观众可透过孔洞看到一面砖墙上的大洞。再往后则可见一位赤裸女性的躯干截段，以及一只手臂和双腿，这具女性身体仿佛尸体般横卧在树枝与树叶上，剃过毛发的生殖部位暴露出来，她的左臂举着一盏闪亮的煤气灯。

paragraphs.en: For the woman’s body, Duchamp assembled casts of the limbs of his second wife, “Teeny” Sattler—whom he married in 1954 after she left her art-dealer husband, Pierre Matisse (son of Henri)—as well as of two of his lovers: Mary Reynolds, an avant-garde bookbinder, with whom he carried on an affair starting in the 1920s, and the surrealist sculptor Maria Martins, the second great thwarted love of Duchamp’s life, after Buffet-Picabia. They had an intense affair in New York in the ’40s and early ’50s, and Duchamp was devastated when her husband, a Brazilian diplomat, was transferred to Paris and Martins chose to go with him. Étant donnés, which Jasper Johns called “the strangest work of art in any museum,” is a transgressive, deeply disturbing piece that continues to flummox its would-be interpreters. If it functions as a romantic tribute, and perhaps an art-historical one (it most obviously calls to mind Gustave Courbet’s The Origin of the World ), it’s also a creepy, confusing, isolating experience that turns every viewer into a morbid voyeur of a bewildering sex crime.
paragraphs.zh: 在这位女性身体的塑造上，杜尚拼接了其第二任妻子“蒂妮”·萨特勒（Teeny Sattler）的肢体模型，她在1954年嫁给杜尚，当时她刚离开与丈夫——艺术经纪人皮耶尔·马蒂斯（亨利的儿子——）的婚姻。他还用了与两位恋人的肢体模型：玛丽·雷诺兹，一位先锋派装帧师，他在20世纪20年代就与其发生了婚外情；以及超现实主义雕塑家玛丽娅·马丁斯，继布菲特-皮卡比亚之后，杜尚生命中的第二个未竟恋情。她们在20世纪40年代至50年代初期于纽约有过一段激烈的恋情，后来马丁斯的巴西丈夫被调往巴黎，杜尚得知后几乎崩溃不已，因为马丁斯选择随丈夫离去。詹斯珀·约翰斯曾称《Étant donnés》为“任何博物馆里最古怪的艺术作品”，它是一件越界、令人深度不安的作品，至今仍让试图解读的人困惑不已。如果说它是一种浪漫致敬，或许也是一种艺术史致敬（它最明显地令人联想到居斯塔夫·库尔贝的《世界的起源》），它同时又是一种令人毛骨悚然、令人困惑、令人疏离的体验，将每一位观者都变成了一个面对令人错乱的性犯罪场景的病态旁观者。

paragraphs.en: Multiple Portrait of Marcel Duchamp, 1917 (Private Collection, France / © 2026 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York / ADAGP, Paris / Estate of Marcel Duchamp)
paragraphs.zh: 马塞尔·杜尚《多重肖像》（1917） （法国私人收藏 / © 2026 ARS（美国纽约艺术家版权协会），ADAGP（巴黎），杜尚遗产）

paragraphs.en: “Our interest’s on the dangerous edge of things,” Robert Browning wrote in his dramatic monologue “Bishop Blougram’s Apology.” “The honest thief, the tender murderer, / the superstitious atheist.” Had Browning encountered Duchamp, he might have added, “The shy attention-seeker, the nonchalant fanatic, the heartsick Casanova.”
paragraphs.zh: “我们的兴趣总是停留在危险的边缘，”罗伯特·勃朗宁在他的戏剧性独白《布卢格拉姆主教的辩护》中写道。“诚实的小偷、温柔的杀人犯，／ 迷信的无神论者。”若勃朗宁遇见过杜尚，也许还会再补上一句：“害羞的追求注意者、若无其事的狂热者、伤心的卡萨诺瓦。”

paragraphs.en: Duchamp was interested in the dangerous edge of art—that zone of uncertainty where the fiction of the “aesthetic” drops away and the purpose of art grows blurry. In “The Creative Act,” a brief speech he delivered in Houston in 1957, he said that “the creative act is not performed by the artist alone” but also by the spectator, whose interpretations bring the work to life. By dissolving the barrier between art and life and emphasizing the role of the viewer, Duchamp unraveled art’s claim to special status. Suddenly, anything could be art and anyone could make it. Indeed, you were making it just by looking at it.
paragraphs.zh: 杜尚对艺术的危险边界特别着迷——那片不确定性地带：所谓“审美”幻象消散、艺术目的变得模糊的地方。在他于1957年在休斯敦发表的一段简短演讲《创作行为》中，他说“创造行为并非由艺术家单独完成”，而是由观众共同完成，观众的阐释赋予作品生命。通过打破艺术与生活之间的隔膜，并强调观众的作用，杜尚瓦解了艺术作为一种特殊地位的主张。突然之间，任何事物都可能是艺术，任何人都能创造艺术。实际上，你光是凝视它，就已经在创造了。

paragraphs.en: Stripping away the aura of preciousness around art proved enormously liberating. But it also set up a quandary: If art is undifferentiated from life—if it is an arbitrary designation—why even maintain it as a separate category? If a bicycle wheel or a snow shovel can be art, what is the point of exhibiting these objects in galleries? Why not contemplate them (if that’s what you wish to do) in the bicycle-repair shop or the basement? Why have “art” at all?
paragraphs.zh: 把艺术周围那层珍贵光环剥离，带来了巨大的解放感。但这也带来了一个难题：如果艺术与生活没有本质区别——如果它只是一个任意的命名——那为什么还要把它作为一个独立范畴来保留？如果一只自行车轮胎或一把雪铲都能是艺术，那么在画廊里展出这些物件又有什么意义？为什么不在自行车修理店或地下室里去细看它们（如果你真想那样做）？为什么还要有“艺术”呢？

paragraphs.en: Duchamp’s habitual skepticism had the salutary effect of returning us to first principles. For him, part of being modern—living in the aftermath of Darwin, Freud, Nietzsche, and the trenches of World War I—meant dispensing with inherited illusions. “All this twaddle, existence of God, atheism, determinism, free will, societies, death, etc. are the pieces of a game of chess called language,” he once wrote in a letter. “Art,” by his reckoning, was merely another word, a piece in the game.
paragraphs.zh: 杜尚一贯的怀疑主义，反而使我们回到第一性原理。对他来说，所谓现代性——生活在达尔文、弗洛伊德、尼采以及第一次世界大战战壕之后——意味着摆脱先天继承的幻觉。他曾在一封信中写道：“这一切胡言乱语，神的存在、无神论、决定论、自由意志、社会、死亡等等，都是语言这盘棋游戏中的棋子。”在他看来，“艺术”也只是另一个词罢了，是棋局中的一颗子。

paragraphs.en: Busy scorching communal “fictions,” Duchamp became ever more absorbed by private concerns that could often be impenetrable. If he was forever puzzling over what art is and isn’t, he was also endlessly puzzling over the relationship between sex and love. Averse to sentimentality, he didn’t really believe in romantic love. But being intellectually skeptical of something is not the same as banishing it from one’s existence. If Duchamp remained, in spite of everything, an artist, he also remained a man prone to falling in love. “Contradiction,” he quipped near the end of his life, “is the whole point.”
paragraphs.zh: 当他忙于烧尽这些公共性的“虚构”时，杜尚越来越沉迷于那些常常难以洞察的私人困惑。如果他一直在揪心“什么是艺术、什么不是艺术”，他同样也不断困惑于性与爱之间的关系。厌恶矫情的他，并不真正相信浪漫之爱。但对某事持理性怀疑，并不等于把它从自身生命中驱逐。若杜尚最终仍是一个艺术家，那么他也始终是一个容易坠入爱河的人。他在生命晚期打趣道，“矛盾，正是全部要点。”
